title: Building AI Copilots
lesson:
A copilot is an AI helper that lives inside the tool you're already using and helps you do your work, in context, without taking over. Think of GitHub Copilot suggesting the next line of code, a writing app drafting the rest of your email, or a spreadsheet tool offering to fill a column. The name is the whole idea: in a plane, the copilot sits beside the pilot, hands on nothing unless asked, suggesting and assisting while the pilot stays in command. You — the human — keep flying the plane.

Copilot vs chatbot vs agent

These three get muddled, so let's separate them. A chatbot is a conversation in a separate window: you go to it, ask, get an answer, come back. An agent is given a goal and runs off on its own, looping until done. A copilot sits between them: it's embedded in your real work (your document, your code editor, your CRM) and it's human-in-the-loop — it suggests, you accept, edit, or reject. The phrase "human-in-the-loop" just means a person reviews and approves each meaningful step before anything sticks.

CopilotAgent
Lives inside your existing appOften its own process or chat
You approve every suggestionActs on its own toward a goal
Knows what you're looking at right nowPlans a multi-step path itself
Low risk, low surpriseMore power, more ways to go wrong

The secret ingredient: context

A copilot feels magic only when it knows what you're doing right now. Here's the second analogy: a good copilot is like a great shop assistant who walks the floor with you. A useless one shouts the same script at every customer. A great one notices the shoes in your hand, your size, the rain outside — and then suggests. That "what you're holding" is context: the file you have open, the cell you clicked, the text around your cursor, who you are, what you did a minute ago. Gathering that context and stuffing the relevant bits into the prompt is 80% of the work. The model is the same one anyone can call; your edge is the context you feed it.

Because context is huge and the context window (how much text the model can read at once) is limited, you rarely dump everything. You retrieve the relevant slices — often with RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation, pulling in matching documents) — and assemble a focused prompt:

def build_copilot_prompt(user, cursor_text, open_file, recent_edits):
    docs = retrieve_relevant(query=cursor_text, top_k=5)  # RAG: pull matching snippets
    return [
        {"role": "system", "content": COPILOT_RULES},     # behaviour + output format
        {"role": "user", "content": (
            f"User: {user.name}, role {user.role}\n"
            f"File: {open_file.path}\n"
            f"Relevant docs:\n{docs}\n"
            f"Recent edits:\n{recent_edits}\n"
            f"Cursor is here; continue:\n{cursor_text}"
        )},
    ]

Notice there's no fine-tuning here. Most copilots are not custom-trained models — they're an off-the-shelf model plus excellent prompt engineering and context. You only reach for fine-tuning when you need a very specific style or format the prompt can't reliably get.

Design rules that make or break it

  • Latency is a feature. An inline code suggestion that takes 4 seconds is useless — the user has typed past it. Copilots that complete text need answers in well under a second, so people lean on smaller, faster models (see SLMs) and the latency-vs-quality trade-off matters more than raw smarts.
  • Always reversible. Suggestions should be easy to reject with one keystroke. The cost of a bad suggestion must be near zero, or people switch the copilot off.
  • Show, don't do. Propose a diff, a ghosted completion, a draft — let the human commit it. The moment a copilot silently changes things, trust collapses.
  • Stable output shape. If your UI expects a JSON diff or a single line, the model must return exactly that, every time. Function calling or strict schemas keep the output parseable.

Real-world examples in 2026: coding editors that complete functions and explain errors; CRM (customer-relationship-management) copilots that draft the follow-up email using the customer's history; spreadsheet copilots that propose a formula for the column you just started; design tools that suggest the next layout. The thread running through all of them is the same — be where the user already is, know their current context, suggest fast, and let the human stay the pilot. Get those four right and the copilot feels like a quiet, capable colleague rather than a gimmick.
mentalModel: A copilot is the assistant in the passenger seat, not the autopilot. It lives inside the tool you're already using, watches what you're doing right now, and offers fast, easy-to-reject suggestions — while you keep your hands on the controls and approve every move.
mistakes: Ignoring latency. A brilliant suggestion that arrives 3 seconds late is worse than no suggestion — the user has moved on. For inline completions, speed beats cleverness; pick a faster model and trim the prompt before chasing quality.

Feeding generic context. Sending the same boilerplate prompt for every user makes the copilot a parrot. The whole value is knowing the open file, the cursor, the recent edits — gather real context or you've built a worse chatbot.

Letting it act without approval. Silently editing the user's document or sending an email destroys trust the first time it's wrong. Keep it human-in-the-loop: propose a diff or draft, let the person commit it.

Unstructured output your UI can't parse. If the editor needs a clean diff or one line but the model wraps it in prose and markdown fences, the feature breaks. Pin the output with a strict schema or function calling.

Stuffing the whole codebase or document into the prompt. It blows the context window, costs a fortune, and buries the relevant bit. Retrieve the few relevant slices (use RAG) instead of sending everything.

Reaching for fine-tuning too early. Most copilots are an off-the-shelf model plus great context and prompting. Try that first; only fine-tune when you need a specific style or format that prompting can't reliably produce.
exercise:
Build a tiny "commit-message copilot" as a command-line script. It should take a code diff (paste one in, or read git diff output), send it to a model with a system prompt that says "You write one concise commit message in the imperative mood; output only the message, nothing else," and print the suggestion. Crucially, make it human-in-the-loop: after printing, ask Accept? [y/n/edit] and only "commit" (just print 'committed') if the user types y. This is the copilot shape in miniature — context (the diff) in, fast suggestion out, human approves.

Stretch goal: Add real context-gathering: include the names of the changed files and the current branch in the prompt, and measure how long the model takes to respond. If it's over ~1 second, swap to a smaller/faster model and compare the suggestion quality — you'll feel the latency-vs-quality trade-off firsthand.
solution: The core loop is short: read the diff, build messages with a tight system prompt that forces a single clean line, call the model, print the result, then read the user's keypress and branch on it. The two lessons to internalise: first, the system prompt is doing the heavy lifting — "output only the message" is what keeps the result pasteable, exactly like a real copilot needs parseable output. Second, the Accept? [y/n/edit] step is the human-in-the-loop design; without it you've built an agent that commits on its own, which is a different, riskier thing. For the stretch goal, wrapping the model call in a timer makes latency concrete. You'll likely find a small model returns a perfectly good commit message in a fraction of the time, which is exactly why production copilots so often pick the faster model for short, high-frequency suggestions and save the big model for rare, hard requests.
summary: A copilot is an AI assistant embedded in the tool you already use, suggesting in-context and keeping the human in the loop — between a standalone chatbot and a self-driving agent. Its power comes less from the model and more from the context you feed it and tight prompting, not fine-tuning. Win by being fast (latency is a feature), reversible (easy to reject), and structured (parseable output your UI can use).

keyTerms.def: An AI assistant embedded inside an existing application that offers in-context suggestions while the human stays in control and approves each step.
A design where a person reviews and approves the AI's suggestions before anything takes effect, rather than letting it act unsupervised.
The information about what the user is doing right now — open file, cursor position, recent actions, identity — that you gather and put into the prompt.
A proposal shown right where the user is working, like ghosted text or a code completion, that they accept or dismiss with a keystroke.
The delay between request and response; for copilots it must be very low (often under a second) or the suggestion arrives too late to be useful.
Forcing the model to return a fixed, parseable shape (a single line, a diff, JSON) so the surrounding app can use it reliably.
Pulling in the most relevant documents or snippets and adding them to the prompt, so the copilot answers using the right context without sending everything.
The principle that any copilot suggestion must be cheap and easy to undo, so a wrong guess costs the user almost nothing.
